以下是来自Arlyeon进行的脑缸组专访文字版，我们取得Arlyeon授权后，将会把文本版分成四部分持续发布在《忘却前夜》Discord频道内，完整视频版请点击下方链接前往收看~ The following is the text version of the Brain Vat Group interview conducted by Arlyeon. With Arlyeon's authorization, we will be releasing the text version in four parts on the "Morimens" Discord channel. For the full video version, please click the link below to watch~

 https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-Vsd5hUMAT8

embeds.title: Surviving Oblivion - Talking Morimens W/ Light & Jiyue Of Brain In ...
embeds.description:
So, after many a year, I finally dipped my toes into #morimens - a #lovecraftianhorror fueled #roguelite #deckbuilder - and in doing so, wound up with a *lot* of questions.

Questions I was fortunately able to get answered, given that their Studio is fairly friendly. So, join me as I find out more a

content:
> ***Part III 关于忘却前夜的玩法/机制***

> ***Q31***
> 如今的《忘却前夜》与团队最初构想相比有多大不同？有没有原本想加入但最终砍掉的机制？最难打磨的内容是什么？

    ***A31***
《Morimens》早期构想与现在差异很大。当时的暂定名是《代号：密境》：叙事会更侧重校内生活、重复探索“密境”、逐步解密真相；美术上计划做更具象化的弥萨格大学与关卡场景，用视听直接增强沉浸感；玩法上“唤醒体”只决定初始卡牌，战斗过程中能够有机会将这些卡牌变成不同功能的变种并不断增强，探索中也能收集界域通用卡牌来构建卡组。

这些愿景至今仍让我心动，但更高的美术质量要求、难以复用的资产、更难落地的叙事结构，以及偏离市场规律的游戏时长与商业模式，都让这个愿景以当时的情况来说并不现实。初始团队曾为此投入两年，但完成度不尽人意，这也是团队在 2021 年迎来首次危机的重要原因之一。后来为了让游戏能够优先完成，游戏的设计方案一步一步演化为现在的面貌。

机制层面，我曾想加入一个独特“疯狂量表”：它会随探索选择与遭遇分段变化，在最终阶段赋予更危险但更强的力量。这类设计思路也常见于《无间冥寺》等克苏鲁题材的作品，其中我尤其喜欢《黑暗地牢》里压力系统带来的张力：多数时候是崩溃与连锁破坏，少数时候也会激发英雄的正面品质、实现逆转——我们想要的也是这种“感受比例”：整体背景虽然黑暗压抑，但仍然会突出人性的微光。

但最终这个想法被简化为现在的“症状卡”。首先是因为担心玩家的理解负担：现有系统已经包含守密人、四唤醒体与四界域的组队结构，以及命轮、密契、启灵、技能、等级等养成，再叠加探索内的造物、刻印、黑印等要素，复杂度很高；继续增加通用量表会显著抬高理解门槛。其次是因为关卡长度与叙事节奏：故事模式的探索流程被压缩且关卡长短会随叙事变化，量表很难有足够颗粒度体现深度，更适合纯肉鸽。第三则是因为负面反馈强度已经太高：策略难度与叙事节奏本就偏沉，继续强化负面机制可能导致体验“负面过载”。

更关键的是人物塑造的适配度：守密人是在绝望中仍选择直面不可名状的少数人，唤醒体则是协助其对抗疯狂的强大同伴。我们不希望用一种常见的基础机制让他们频繁处于崩溃或疯狂；即便唤醒体借助疯狂变强，也应当是更主动、更可控的选择，例如“灵知觉醒”。不同作品如《黑暗地牢》或者最近推出的《卡厄斯梦境》在叙事与机制取向上显然与《忘却前夜》不同，这并无高下之分，只是不同作品的选择。顺带一提，《卡厄斯梦境》在探索中强化卡牌变化方面很出色，战斗设计师米酒就沉迷过一段时间，深受启发。

最难打磨的部分，是把叙事、探索、战斗三种不同层次整合成连贯一致的体验：三者切换时很容易丢失当前目标与动机。为此团队做了大量整合、取舍并进行多轮打磨。例如第一部第二章詹金的特殊关卡，几乎放弃传统战斗的策略与养成，把战斗转为第一视角体验的表达工具；第一部决战则是集大成：守密人失去能力与记忆、再逐步找回，音乐、剧情与机制汇合，让玩家的记忆与情感在那一刻合为一体。

> ***Q32***
> 星辰篇版本进行了大量平衡调整，例如为中毒、反击设置上限，让升华叠堆不再无解。你们平衡调整的核心理念是什么？团队规模对平衡工作有何影响？你们与社区合作到什么程度才达成满意版本？还有哪些内容仍有优化空间？

  ***  A32***
我们游戏玩法设计的核心理念主要有三个：

    ① 尽可能让玩法与叙事统一。一张卡牌不只是“机制本身”，而是某位唤醒体能力的组成部分；它所表达的也不仅是数值强弱，还包括机制、数值、环境与其他角色联动关系共同构成的“体验叙事”。这种思路虽然在一定程度上牺牲了纯粹理性、追求机制分布美学的设计，但确实为角色的玩法体验注入了独特的灵魂。比如环行·拉蒙娜能够复制上一回合最后打出的卡牌，就对应她“从过去经历中获得力量”的特质。

    ② 尽可能让每个选择都有趣。无论队伍怎么组、造物怎么选、先打哪张牌，都要能形成不同且有意义的策略取舍。为此，每新增一个机制或一张卡牌，都需要尽可能覆盖其与既有内容的组合关系，并为其赋予策略价值；但与此同时，它又会反过来影响大量已有组合。这个理念会把团队不断拉向巨大的工作量深渊，实际上也很难做到完美，但也正因为此，它塑造了本作独特的策略风味和自由度。即便是早期的免费角色，战斗设计师也会通过克制、组合、环境、强化等方式，尽量让他们始终有用武之地。

    ③ 尽可能保持“只增强、不削弱”。《忘却前夜》不是买断制或 PVP 竞技为核心的游戏；从抽卡商业模式的角度来看，它本质更像一种持续服务承诺。玩家非常不希望开发团队削弱自己通过大量投入获得的能力。如果战斗设计师们觉得确实不得不进行削弱，我们也会提供退还机制，让玩家能够重新投资——这当然会影响收入，但这是必须承担的代价。游戏在第一年里发生过几次削弱调整，给大家带来了不好的体验；后来我们从中吸取教训，转而更多通过调整环境来解决问题，并最终回调了当时的一些修改。

由于战斗设计师不可能在持续扩展的前提下真正穷尽所有可能，在同时坚守设计理念并支持商业化设计时，就必然会导致一定的机制与数值膨胀。比如“狂气爆发”如果变成每回合都能稳定使用，它就会失去应有的张力，也会让“狂气”本身变得不再重要。这就导致战斗设计师需要基于坚守原则 ， 引入星辰篇中的这类环境机制，把“选择”重新变得有趣——例如影响狂气获取与使用的环境机制、或者为反击与中毒这类机制设置堆叠上限。这并不是为了为难玩家，而是设计师在“游戏乐趣与服务承诺”之间做取舍后必须采取的方式。我也很羡慕《杀戮尖塔》这类买断作品能够更灵活的通过增强和削弱来使游戏玩法达到几乎完美；而《忘却前夜》则需要更动态、也更曲折的方式不断尝试接近这个目标。

团队规模的提升，意味着战斗设计师能投入更多时间在设计与测试上，从而在设计阶段就尽量减少不合理隐患：既让新内容保持足够吸引力，又尽量不破坏既有平衡，甚至反过来改善环境。比如近期诞妄·墨菲、茉夏以及徐的推出，就让一些早期角色获得了更大的发挥空间，也更容易呈现出合理的数值强度。在社区协作层面，我们会持续关注社区反馈与数据统计，它们能帮助战斗设计师确认设计是否达成预期，也能暴露设计阶段可能遗漏掉的问题，从而为后续的迭代指明方向。目前我认为最值得优化的内容，是进一步强化探索过程中造物的玩法影响力：让它们更独特、更有趣，也让每次探索带来更多惊喜。

> ***Q33***
> 除了角色堆叠启灵、命轮等通用养成模块，你们是否计划加入其他角色养成系统？未来是否会出现更多角色专属模块化养成，提供横向养成选择，而非单纯需要肝练度的纵向强度提升？

***A33***
计划中确实有一套面向常驻唤醒体的额外养成内容。出于商业模式的合理性考虑，限定唤醒体相较于常驻唤醒体，拥有约等同于 10 级的基础属性优势；但从长期来看，我们也希望常驻唤醒体能够消耗“无垢之芯”去逐步弥补这部分差距，让大家更有理由去游玩自己喜欢的常驻唤醒体，而不必总是追随限定唤醒体。预计这个功能很快就能与大家见面了！

除此以外，暂时没有更多加入养成系统的计划。不过随着游戏发展到某个阶段、需要新的长期目标去鼓励大家体验新内容时，也不排除会这么做。届时我们会尽可能保持克制，来平衡好压力感。

**Q34
主线已预告癫狂模式，这个模式开发中最大的挑战与障碍是什么？你们计划如何让选择体验该模式的玩家觉得物有所值？**

A34
癫狂模式真正的设计挑战，在于大家不希望它只是数值版本的困难模式——单纯提高推荐等级、再用奖励驱动重复。它需要带来真正的体验变化：可能是敌人行为逻辑的调整，可能是该难度下通用规则的改变，也可能是某些章节专属的机制变体。这意味着每个章节的癫狂模式几乎都需要独立设计，而不能套用一套通用公式——这是主要的设计难点。理想情况下，癫狂模式的挑战性与新体验本身就是奖励，而不是反过来变成"不完成就亏了"的压力。

至于为什么暂时没有排进近期日程——这是因为我们观察到困难模式在多次增加奖励后整体完成率不及预期。是不是困难模式本身就存在问题，现在推出癫狂模式是否是合适的时机？是这个模式不够好玩、还是难度要求对当前玩家来说过高？这些问题的答案，会直接影响战斗设计师们如何设计癫狂模式。

> ***Q35***
> 除了融灾禁区之外，第二部几乎舍弃了探索玩法。你们是否计划把它加回来，或是改版以带来更多样化的体验？

***A35***
第二部重启时，团队正处在最脆弱的阶段。在那种条件下，我重新审视了探索玩法本身：它的联合设计难度高、复用性低，玩家熟悉后有意义的分支选择也并不算多——这些问题在第一部时就存在，只是之前没有被迫正视。既然如此，与其在新的部分里重复一个有结构性问题的模式，不如先移除它，看看没有它的第二部会是什么体验——最后的结果是好坏参半，有玩家反馈失去了一些调查的风味，也有玩家反馈战斗爽快了很多。

也许更合适的做法是在一些关键叙事节点，我们以更克制的方式引入探索玩法——让它在需要它的地方出现，而不是作为每章的固定结构。除此之外，战斗设计师也会在未来的其他玩法模式中尝试变化更丰富的全新探索形式。

> ***Q36***
> 你们还有想要加入的新机制吗？无论是在第二部后续、可能的第三部，还是主线之外的其他玩法模式中。

***A36***
通常而言，并没有一份所谓的“待加入机制清单”。战斗设计师们的设计方式是：以既定的设计理念为指导，来一步一步推导机制与数值的取舍。

比如我们让萝坦成为“打击流”，并不是因为我们需要在玩法里硬加入一个打击流派，而是因为萝坦的能力与人设很适合用打击的手感与循环去表达；因此我们先为萝坦创造了这套玩法，并在后续遇到同样适配的角色与情境时，再基于这个概念进行延伸。

例如希洛与萝坦有一种“宿命感”，所以我们让希洛成为游戏中第二个打击流；又因为莉莉与希洛的能力来源设定，我们让莉莉也具备打击能力。
因为莉莉其忍耐痛苦的经历，我们也为她加入了“忍耐”机制。而这些角色的流派与玩法感受，也会在他们的敌人版本、以及“相位对弈”等玩法中形成呼应，尽量保持一致性。

这一理念不仅适用于角色，也适用于界域与章节层面的机制设计：例如“超维”强调对时空的掌控感，“深海”强调对触腕的号令能力；章节共鸣也是类似的——第七章共鸣体现“孕育”，24 的特遣纪录共鸣体现“人格分裂、缺失而后重新归一”，萨尔瓦多的特遣纪录共鸣体现“负罪前行”。

因此，我们不一定会先“想要加入某个新机制”，再去寻找它的落点；但只要我们还有新的角色需要塑造、有新的故事需要讲述，就会自然产生与之匹配的新机制，并把它们以合适的形式带进主线或其他玩法模式中。

> ***Q37***
> 最近实装的列车玩法提供了日常可体验的内容。你们是否计划加入更多日常、周常内容，让玩家在无新主线时也有内容可玩？当玩家拿完成就和点数奖励后，如何让这些玩法继续提供正向反馈？

***A37***
我们确实有计划加入更多可持续游玩、耐玩的内容，让大家在没有新主线可读的阶段也有东西可玩。
例如，大家希望有一个更耐玩、也更有辨识度的长流程肉鸽式卡牌构筑玩法——这其实也是《忘却前夜》最初的玩法起点。此前为了减少叙事上的中断感、并适配移动端节奏，我做过一些妥协；但如果把长肉鸽放在"特殊玩法"里引入，会非常合适。它最大的挑战在于：如何让大量基于短流程设计的机制与数值曲线，能够自然延展到长流程里，且全过程仍然保持有趣。这次推出的列车玩法，就是一次偏DEMO级的尝试：它还有很多不足，但至少验证了方向是成立的。

除了卡牌玩法之外，我也想尝试引入音乐玩法。我非常喜欢《Deemo》这样的音乐节奏游戏，能让我在完成节奏的同时沉浸于音乐情感之中；也很喜欢像《八方旅人》这样优秀的原声集——当某段旋律响起，某个剧情片段、某个人物的关键抉择往往会在脑海里重新浮现。很幸运我们拥有 Frank 这样的作曲家，为《Morimens》创作了数百首动人的曲目；希望有一天大家能够在游戏里以另一种全新的方式欣赏这些音乐！

此外，我们的战斗设计师米酒也在尝试一些更休闲、但同样耐玩的肉鸽玩法方向，让玩家不必总处在高度思考的紧张状态中，而是获得另一种放松方式、但仍有不同的玩法乐趣。

至于"如何在拿完成就与点数奖励后仍保持正向反馈"：我们的思路是，前期会用一些简单任务引导大家上手，再通过少量的"不同构筑或组合"目标与轻度难度挑战，鼓励大家探索玩法深度，最后再以点数里程碑作为阶段性目标。但到这个程度之后，就不再继续用大量物质奖励来吸引玩家——"错过就很亏"的压力必须被控制在合理范围内。在奖励趋于收束之后，玩法能否继续提供正反馈，必须更多依赖玩法本身的耐玩性。就像相位对弈，很多玩家即便没有额外奖励也会反复游玩，因为它本身足够有吸引力。
《忘却前夜》不太可能像《FF7 Rebirth》这样的旗舰级作品那样，在游戏里加入大量迷你游戏来丰富玩家的体验。每加入一种新玩法，我都必须更谨慎地评估它的可维护性与耐玩性，确保投入产出比。另一方面，战斗设计师们也必须警惕不切实际的幻想——不存在一种"永远玩不腻、还能快速量产很多个"的终极玩法。因此我们的目标，是在"内容供给"与"可维护、耐玩"之间找到更平衡的解法。

> ***Q38***
> 相位对弈提供了PVP体验，但游戏几乎没有主动合作玩法。你们是否尝试合作玩法的计划？例如用相位对弈技术做团本BOSS战？如果有，实现这个想法最大的阻碍是什么？

***A38***
我们确实考虑过开发公会合作玩法。但我们希望它不只是“有合作的外壳”，而是在玩法层面也足够好玩；同时尽量避免它变成一种仅靠奖励驱动、伴随强社交压力的周期性打卡任务。

目前最大的挑战是在合作玩法的设计中如何让不同策略水平、不同养成深度的玩家都能找到合适的参与方式，在协作中获得乐趣，并且以《忘却前夜》的独特卡牌战斗玩法保持长期耐玩。

至于相位对弈的技术框架是否能延伸到合作玩法，是考虑过的方向之一；但团队合作场景下的平衡逻辑与对抗场景存在根本差异，因此不适合直接复用。

倒是两人合作这个规模值得优先尝试——相比大型团队副本，它的平衡复杂度更低，也更容易在卡牌战斗框架里找到有趣的协作设计空间。

> ***Q39***
> 《忘却前夜》从一开始就只计划做4个界域吗？还是曾经有过更多界域的计划，未来也可能加入新界域？

***A39***
在2021年重做整体设计时，确实设想过不止四个界域；但这些"额外界域"大多只是埋在早期剧情里的伏笔，没有进入实装阶段。

控制界域数量的核心判断是：界域数量与每个界域的可用角色数量成反比。界域一旦过多，每个界域内的唤醒体就会过少，玩家的组队选择和养成深度都会受损——这是结构性的设计约束。

另一个早期的设计障碍是：当时几乎不允许跨界域混编，"选界域"的绑定感极强——类似于《魔兽世界》里选择联盟还是部落，一旦选定，就会在很长时间里决定你的组队与玩法体验。这种割裂感直到2.0开放界域混编后才得到明显缓解。

目前在第二部阶段，我们没有引入新界域的计划。未来如果游戏发展到合适的阶段，新界域并非完全没有可能——但前提是既有界域的内容密度已经足够，新界域的加入不会稀释而是丰富整体体验。

> ***Q40***
> 24是游戏中非常独特的唤醒体，能为所有界域提供加成，某种意义上属于所有界域。你们是否计划加入其他像她一样属于多个阵营的唤醒体？

***A40***
「24」绝对是“例外中的例外”。无论是战斗中的双形态切换、编队时随队伍而变化的“四界域适配”，还是特遣纪录里“分裂然后找回”的共鸣机制——本质上都是为了服务她“人格分裂”的叙事塑造。甚至连她的试玩关卡和奖励都有四份。

正如此前提到的角色设计理念：我们并不是因为想给游戏强行加入一个“适配四界域”的机制，才做出了「24」；恰恰相反，是因为要把「24」这个角色成立地呈现出来，才为她量身定制了这套机制。

这几乎等于把“四个角色的生态位”压缩进同一个角色里，会显著挤占后续的玩法设计空间，从商业理性角度也并不划算。但在“尽可能让玩法与叙事统一”的设计理念下，战斗设计师仍然选择了这样做——即使前面做了很多现实层面的妥协，也总得有一些时刻，为理想燃烧一次。

从结果来看，这套设计也确实顺带解决了当时环境中的一些问题：比如作为混沌界域的输出定位，如何与不同界域产生联动，并呈现出不同的游玩风格。但归根结底，这仍然是“尽可能让每个选择都有趣”的设计理念所驱动、自然推导出来的成果。

因此，目前没有再做同类“多界域归属”唤醒体的计划——除非未来出现某个新角色，其叙事表达确实需要用这种机制才能成立。

> ***Q41***
> 目前阶段，你们是否计划调整或加强常驻唤醒体？比如雷娅，她的血量非常糟糕——对于一个靠挨打输出的角色来说，身板实在太脆。

***A41***
会的。战斗设计师会持续对常驻唤醒体做强化与调整，让他们在当前环境里依然有稳定的上场空间。近期也在规划下一批改动，预计未来几个月会陆续落地。

针对常驻唤醒体在基础属性上的结构性差距，之前也提到过，未来我们会通过额外的系统来弥补这一问题。

当这些内容都完成之后，我们会继续关注雷娅的表现，并做出进一步必要的调整。

> ***Q42***
> 除了利莫里亚阵营内部互相加成之外，你们是否计划加入更多类似阵营加成？还是说这个设计仅用于平衡，不会在其他阵营广泛使用？

***A42***
目前没有“要把阵营加成推广到其他阵营”的明确计划，但从长期来看也并非完全不可能。

阵营加成首先是叙事导向的设计。比如诞妄·墨菲属于利莫里亚，但墨菲却不属于——这与她们各自对利莫里亚的态度与身份认同密切相关，因此我们希望通过机制把这种差异呈现出来。

从最终的玩法效果来看，这种设计也带来了额外的平衡收益：一方面避免了“诞妄·墨菲 + 墨菲”的献祭组合演变成过于显眼的单一最优解；另一方面也提升了利莫里亚体系整体的上场空间。但这些结果依然是从“尽可能让每个选择都有意义”的设计理念出发，自然推导出的副产物，而不是单纯为了平衡而强行加上的限制。

如果阵营加成做得过强，“把阵营加成拉满”就会变成唯一正确答案，玩家会明显感到被“设计师教你玩”，从而损失构筑自由度。这需要在强度、叙事、自由度上始终保持克制与平衡。

> ***Q43***
> 你们是否计划加入更多福利角色，让玩家几乎不用氪金、只花时间就能培养？顺带一问，是否有更多容易获取的本源角色？例如本源罗坦，无论通过第二部主线还是特遣纪录实装。

***A43***
目前没有新增“非抽卡获取”的唤醒体计划；未来游戏会有更多本源角色，但很遗憾不会有更多像环行·拉蒙娜这样、具备特殊获取方式的本源唤醒体的计划。

环行·拉蒙娜之所以采用特殊获取机制，是“叙事优先”的结果：拉蒙娜与环行·拉蒙娜在设定上共用同一具身体，因此即便通过助战等方式，也不会出现她们同时出现在同一支队伍里的情况。基于这种设定约束，她也就不适合采用“通过抽取获得”的投放形式。

不过，我们会尽可能在合理范围内提高游戏内可获得的奖励，并结合社区活动或一些纪念性质的非常规福利，让不氪金玩家也能拥有更多获取限定唤醒体、并更轻松完成养成的机会；也会持续更新交换商店的奖励供给。

虽然没办法像一些抽卡游戏一样长期为大家提供新的“福利唤醒体”，但我们会尽力确保大家即使不付费，也依然能够以相对健康的节奏获得完整且有乐趣的游戏体验！

> ***Q44***
> 随着游戏持续运营，你们是否计划提升守密人等级上限？

***A44***
最早，守密人等级是用来展示玩家进入游戏后期之前的“积累进度”，上限相对容易达成，这样大家到顶之后就不必再为等级本身分心。

但随着越来越多的玩家陆续达到等级上限，有不少人反馈希望继续开放更高的等级。因此系统设计师重新审视了它的定位：将它变成一种清晰、可见的长期积累，所以后面游戏内提升过 2 次守密人等级上限。

未来如果随着游戏的发展确实有需要，这仍然可能发生。但我们会谨慎控制由此带来的奖励、内容解锁与强度压力，使之对大多数玩家而言是合理、可接受的。

> ***Q45***
> 你们是否计划让玩家自定义登录界面？我个人真的很喜欢泰旖丝的登录界面。

***A45***
也有一些玩家跟我说，泰旖丝的这个登录画面“震撼过头”了，想能自定义换回更早期的登录画面（笑）。

关于“自定义登录界面”这个需求，其实社区中已经反馈过多次了，只是之前技术成员一直忙于其他开发事务，所以暂时还没来得及处理。
既然你这次又提到了，那我就正式把它作为一个小功能加入日程吧——请再耐心等一等，会有的！

> Part III Questions About Morimens Gameplay / Mechanically
> 
> ***Q31***
How different is Morimens from what your team originally envisioned, vs the state of the game today? Were there specific mechanics you wanted to incorporate that you cut? What were the hardest elements to hammer out? 

***A31***
The early concept for Morimens was vastly different from what it is today. Our initial working title was Codename: Domain. In that version, the narrative was centered more on campus life, with players repeatedly exploring a mysterious "Domain" to gradually uncover the truth. Visually, we planned to build highly detailed representations of Mythag University and its level environments, using audiovisual elements to heighten immersion directly. 

Mechanically, your Awakeners only determined your starting hand; during battle, you would have opportunities to transform those cards into different functional variants and continuously enhance them, while also collecting Realm-specific cards during exploration to build your deck.

These early visions still captivate me, but they ultimately proved unrealistic given our circumstances at the time. The demands for high-fidelity art, the lack of reusable assets, a narrative structure that was difficult to execute, and a gameplay duration and business model that strayed too far from market norms all stood in the way. Our initial team spent two years on this path, but the results were unsatisfactory — this was one of the primary reasons we faced our first major crisis in 2021. To ensure the game could actually reach completion, the design gradually evolved, step by step, into what it is today.

On a mechanics level, we frequently discussed incorporating a unique "Madness Scale" in our early concepts. This would have shifted in segments based on your choices and encounters during exploration, granting dangerous but powerful abilities in its final phase — a concept common in Cthulhu Mythos-inspired works like Curse of the Dead Gods. I've always been particularly drawn to the tension created by the stress system in Darkest Dungeon: most of the time it leads to breakdowns and disastrous chain reactions, but occasionally it sparks a hero's positive traits and turns things around. 

That sense of proportion is exactly what we were after — an overall dark and oppressive backdrop that still finds ways to highlight the rare glimmers of humanity.

Ultimately, this idea was simplified into the current "Symptom Cards." First, we were concerned about the cognitive burden on players: the existing system already includes team-building with a Keeper and four Awakeners across four Realms, along with progression systems like the Wheel of Destiny, Covenants, Enlighten, and skill levels — and on top of that, exploration elements like relics, orisons, and black sigils. 

Adding a universal Madness Scale on top of all this would have significantly raised the barrier to entry. Second, there was the issue of stage length and narrative pacing: the story mode's exploration flow is more condensed, and stage lengths vary according to the plot, meaning a madness scale would struggle to have enough granularity to feel meaningful — it would be better suited for a pure roguelike. Third, the level of negative feedback was already high enough: the game's strategic difficulty and narrative tone are already quite heavy, and continuing to reinforce punishing mechanics risked pushing the experience into "negative feedback overload."

More critically, it came down to character identity. The Keeper is one of the rare few who chooses to confront the indescribable even in the depths of despair, and the Awakeners are the powerful companions who help them fight back against madness. We didn't want a common core mechanic that forced them into frequent breakdowns or states of madness. Even when Awakeners gain strength through madness, it should be a more proactive, controllable choice — which is what "Gnosis Awakening" represents. 

Games like Darkest Dungeon or the recently released Chaos Zero Nightmare have different narrative and mechanical orientations from Morimens — neither is better than the other, just different creative choices. As an aside, Chaos Zero Nightmare is excellent at enhancing card variations during exploration — our battle designer Mijiu was deeply absorbed in it for a while and found it very inspiring.

The most difficult part has been integrating the three distinct layers of narrative, exploration, and combat into a single, coherent experience: switching between them makes it very easy for players to lose sight of their current goals and motivation. To address this, the team made a great many compromises and went through multiple rounds of polishing.

 For example, the special Jenkin stage in Chapter 2 of the first arc almost entirely abandoned traditional combat strategy and character progression, transforming combat into an expressive tool for a first-person experience.

 The climax of the first arc was the culmination of all this effort: the Keeper loses their abilities and memories, then gradually regains them, as music, story, and mechanics all converge — allowing the player's own memories and emotions to merge with those of the game in that single moment.

> ***Q32***
> Astral Reign saw a lot of balance changes, such as capping poison and counter, as well as ensuring that Exalt spam isn't quite as viable as it used to be. What was the balance Philosophy that guided your hand, how did the size of your team impact your ability to do so — and how closely did you work with the community to reach a state of challenge and mechanical nuance that you could ultimately be satisfied with? Are there still elements that you feel could be improved upon? 

***A32***
The core principles of our gameplay design center around three key ideas:

      ① Unifying Gameplay and NarrativeA card is not just a mechanic in itself — it is a component of an Awakener's abilities. What it expresses goes beyond numerical strength; it forms an "experiential narrative" shaped by the interplay of mechanics, values, the environment, and synergies with other characters. While this approach sacrifices some of the purity of rational, aesthetics-driven mechanical design, it genuinely infuses each character's gameplay experience with a distinct soul. For example, Ramona: Timeworn's ability to replicate the last card played in the previous turn directly mirrors her lore of "drawing strength from past experiences."

      ② Making Every Choice MeaningfulNo matter how you form your team, which relics you choose, or which card you lead with, the goal is to create distinct and meaningful strategic trade-offs.

To achieve this, every new mechanic or card needs to cover as much of its relationship with existing content as possible and be given genuine strategic value — while at the same time inevitably rippling back through a vast web of existing combinations. This philosophy constantly pulls the team toward a "workload abyss" and is incredibly difficult to execute perfectly, but it is precisely what gives the game its unique strategic flavor and sense of freedom. Even for early free-to-play characters, our battle designers work to keep them viable through counters, synergies, environmental factors, and buffs — as much as possible.

      ③ Buffs Only, No Nerfs — As Much as PossibleMorimens is not a buy-to-play or PvP-focused game; from the perspective of a gacha business model, it is essentially an ongoing service commitment. Players are deeply averse to seeing the abilities they have invested heavily in get nerfed. If a nerf becomes truly unavoidable, we will provide a refund mechanism so players can reinvest — this impacts revenue, but it is a cost we must bear. We implemented some nerfs in our first year that resulted in a poor experience for the community; we have since learned from that, shifting toward adjusting the environment to address balance issues instead, and ultimately reverting some of those earlier changes.

Because it is impossible for battle designers to truly exhaust all possibilities in a constantly expanding game, adhering to these principles while supporting commercial design will inevitably lead to some degree of mechanical and numerical inflation. For instance, if "Madness Burst" becomes something that can be reliably triggered every turn, it loses its intended tension and makes "Aliemus" itself feel unimportant. This is why — grounded in Principle 

② — we introduce environmental mechanics like those in the Astral arc to make "choices" feel meaningful again: for example, mechanics that affect how Aliemus is acquired and used, or setting stack caps on mechanics like counterattacks and poison. This is not meant to make things harder for players, but rather a necessary approach the designers must take when balancing game enjoyment against service commitment. I do envy buy-to-play titles like Slay the Spire, which can achieve near-perfection through more flexible use of buffs and nerfs; Morimens must pursue that same goal through a far more dynamic and winding path.

A larger team means battle designers can invest more time in design and testing, minimizing problematic design issues at the design stage itself — keeping new content appealing without disrupting existing balance, and ideally even improving the overall environment. Recent additions like Murphy: Fauxborn, Mouchette, and Xu, for example, have given some early characters more room to shine and made it easier for them to perform at a reasonable power level. On the community collaboration front, we continuously monitor community feedback and data analytics — they help battle designers confirm whether a design is achieving its intended goals, surface issues that may have been missed during the design phase, and point the way for future iterations. What I currently feel most deserves attention is further strengthening the gameplay impact of relics during exploration: making them more unique and engaging, and ensuring every run brings more surprises.

> ***Q33***
> Do you have plans for any other systems for improving a given character, beyond collecting duplicates for them, or the general modular improvements provided by wheels of Destiny. Astral Reign already saw the soul forge — but I was curious if we might see more character focused forms of modular upgrades that could be engaged with, which might provide other means of tweaking how a character performs, and which might lend itself to side grades, rather than out and out upgrades that needed to be grinded up (and which might be pursued independently of wheel/Character Jail). 

***A33***
We do have plans for extra progression content aimed at our Standard Awakeners. Due to considerations of business model viability, Limited Awakeners currently hold a base attribute advantage over Standard Awakeners equivalent to roughly 10 levels. In the long term, however, we want Standard Awakeners to be able to gradually bridge this gap by consuming "Pure Cores" — giving everyone more reason to keep playing the Standard Awakeners they love, rather than always chasing after Limited ones. This feature should be coming very soon!

Beyond that, we have no immediate plans to expand the progression systems further. That said, as the game reaches a certain stage and needs new long-term goals to encourage players to engage with new content, we won't rule it out. If we do go down that path, we will exercise as much restraint as possible to strike the right balance in terms of pressure.

> ***Q34***
> Madness Mode is teased for story missions, what's been the biggest challenge or hurdle with incorporating it — and how do you plan on making it rewarding for players who choose to engage with it?

> ***A34***
> The real design challenge with Madness Mode is that we don't want it to simply be a numerical Hard Mode — just raising the recommended level and driving repetition through rewards. It needs to deliver a genuinely different experience: adjustments to enemy behavior patterns, changes to the global rules at that difficulty level, or chapter-specific mechanic variants. 

This means Madness Mode for each chapter must be designed almost entirely from scratch, without relying on a universal formula — and that is the primary design challenge. Ideally, the challenge and the new experience of Madness Mode should be the reward in themselves, rather than becoming a source of pressure where players feel they've missed out if they can't complete it.

As for why it hasn't been scheduled in the near term — it's because we've observed that Hard Mode's overall completion rate has fallen short of expectations, even after increasing the rewards multiple times. Is there something fundamentally wrong with Hard Mode itself? Is now even the right time to introduce Madness Mode? Is the mode not engaging enough, or is the difficulty simply too high for the current player base? The answers to these questions will directly shape how our battle designers approach the design of Madness Mode.

> ***Q35***
> Exploration has been largely eschewed in Arc 2, beyond D-tide. Are there plans on bringing it back, or possibly changing it up to provide more varied gameplay? 

***A35***
When Arc 2 relaunched, the team was at its most vulnerable. Under those circumstances, I took a step back to re-examine the Exploration gameplay itself: it was difficult to design collaboratively, had low reusability, and offered few meaningful branching choices once players were familiar with it. These issues had existed since Arc 1 — we had simply never been forced to confront them head-on. Given all of this, rather than repeating a structurally flawed model in the new arc, we decided to first remove it and see what Arc 2 would feel like without it. The results were mixed: some players gave feedback that they missed the investigative flavor it brought, while others said combat felt much more satisfying.

Perhaps a more fitting approach would be to reintroduce Exploration in a more restrained way at certain key narrative moments — letting it appear where it's truly needed, rather than treating it as a fixed structural requirement for every chapter. Beyond that, our battle designers will also be experimenting with richer, entirely new forms of exploration in other gameplay modes in the future.

> ***Q36***
> On that subject, are there any mechanics you'd still like to introduce — whether it be later in Arc 2, or during a potential Arc 3? Or even through alternate game play modes accessible outside of the story?

***A36***
In general, we don't work from a "mechanics checklist." Our design approach is guided by our established design principles, deriving trade-offs between mechanics and numerical values step by step.

For example, Lotan wasn't made a "Strike" character because we felt the need to force a Strike playstyle into the game. Rather, it was because Lotan's abilities and character concept were well-suited to being expressed through the feel and loop of Strike gameplay. So we first built this playstyle around her, and then extended the concept when we later encountered characters and situations that fit equally well.

Helot, for instance, shares a sense of shared destiny with Lotan — so she became the second Strike character in the game. And because of the lore connecting Lily's powers to Helot's, Lily was also given Strike capabilities. Because of Lily's experiences of enduring pain, we also introduced the "Endure" mechanic for her. The playstyles and gameplay feel of these characters are echoed in their enemy versions and in modes like "Traphase," maintaining consistency as much as possible.

This philosophy extends beyond individual characters to Realm and Chapter-level mechanics: "Ultra" emphasizes a sense of control over time and space, while "Aequor" emphasizes the ability to command tentacles. Chapter Resonances follow the same logic — the Chapter 7 Resonance embodies "Birth," 24's Special Ops Resonance embodies "Dissociative Identity Disorder, Loss, and Reunification," and Salvador's Special Ops Resonance embodies "Moving Forward Under the Weight of Guilt."

Therefore, we don't necessarily start by deciding we want a new mechanic and then searching for where to put it. But as long as we have new characters to shape and new stories to tell, the mechanics that suit them will emerge naturally — and we will bring them into the main story or other gameplay modes in whatever form feels most fitting.

> ***Q37***
> The recent inclusion of the Train Game Mode provides players with another form of gameplay they can engage with on a day to day basis — do you envision adding a lot more daily and weekly content for players to tackle during the times where they don't have a chapter to chew through? And do you have any thoughts on how to make them feel rewarding even after a player has exhausted the fixed rewards provided through achievements and the overarching point milestones? 

***A37***
We are planning more sustainable, replayable content to keep things interesting between main story updates. Specifically, we want to create a long-form roguelike deck-builder—this actually brings us back to our very first gameplay concepts for the project. 

While we made compromises earlier to suit mobile playstyles and narrative flow, a dedicated "Special Mode" is the perfect place for a deep roguelike experience. The challenge is scaling our short-form mechanics into a long-form journey while keeping it engaging. The "Train" gameplay we recently launched was a "demo-level" test for this; it has flaws, but it proved the direction is viable.

Beyond cards, we are also exploring music-based gameplay. I’m a huge fan of rhythm games like Deemo, and Octopath Traveler, where you get lost in the emotional resonance of the music. We’re lucky to have Frank, our composer, who has written hundreds of beautiful tracks for Morimens. We want to turn that soundtrack into an active, playable experience rather than just background noise. We are also looking into more casual roguelike modes that allow players to relax and step away from high-level strategic thinking while still having fun.

Regarding rewards: our philosophy is to use simple tasks to guide players early on, then encourage deeper play through "build-specific" goals, eventually ending with point milestones. We will not hide massive amounts of upgrade materials behind the hardest content. We want to keep the "missing out" pressure low. Once the materials taper off, the motivation to keep playing should come from the fun of the game itself—much like how players continue to play Traphase just for the enjoyment of the challenge.

Finally, we have to be realistic: we aren't a massive studio that can pack the game with dozens of mini-games like FF7 Rebirth. Every new feature must be sustainable and have a high "replayability-to-cost" ratio.

> ***Q38***
> While Traphase provides players with a means of engaging with PVP, the game doesn't have much in the way of actively collaborative game modes. Is there any intention of experimenting with a co-op form of gameplay? (Experimental Traphase tech used to engage with a raid boss, for instance?) If so, what have been the largest hindrances in executing this sort of idea.

***A38***
We have indeed considered developing cooperative gameplay. However, we want it to be more than just "cooperative in name only" — it needs to be genuinely fun at a mechanical level. At the same time, we want to try as much as possible to avoid it becoming a periodic check-in task driven solely by rewards and accompanied by heavy social pressure.

The biggest challenge right now is figuring out, within the design of cooperative gameplay, how to ensure that players of different levels of strategic skill and different depths of character progression can all find a suitable way to participate — enjoying the collaboration and maintaining the long-term replayability that Morimens' unique card battle gameplay provides.
As for whether the technical framework of Traphase can be extended to cooperative gameplay — that is one of the directions we have considered. However, the balancing logic for team-based cooperative scenarios is fundamentally different from competitive ones, making it unsuitable for direct reuse.

Two-player cooperation, on the other hand, is a scale worth prioritizing as a starting point. Compared to large-scale raids, the balancing complexity is much lower, and it is easier to find interesting cooperative design space within the card battle framework.

> ***Q39***
> Was Morimens always meant to have only 4 realms, or were there ever intentions to have more — or to potentially incorporate another? 

***A39***
When we overhauled the overall design in 2021, we did envision more than four Realms. However, most of these additional Realms remained as bits of foreshadowing buried in the early narrative and never actually made it to the implementation stage.

The core consideration behind limiting the number of Realms is this: the number of Realms is inversely proportional to the number of characters available within each one. If there are too many Realms, each Realm ends up with too few Awakeners, which would compromise players' team-building options and progression depth — this is a structural design constraint.
Another early design obstacle was that cross-realm mixing was almost entirely prohibited at the time.

 The sense of being locked into a Realm was extremely strong — similar to choosing between the Alliance and the Horde in World of Warcraft: once you made that choice, it dictated your team composition and gameplay experience for a long time. This sense of fragmentation wasn't significantly alleviated until cross-realm mixing was introduced in Version 2.0.

Currently, we have no plans to introduce new Realms during the Arc 2 phase. In the future, if the game reaches a suitable stage of development, new Realms are not entirely out of the question — but only on the condition that the content density of the existing Realms is already sufficient, and that the addition of a new Realm would enrich rather than dilute the overall experience.in Version 2.0.

Currently, we have no plans to introduce new Realms during the second phase. While we haven't completely ruled out adding them in the future, it would only happen if the content density of our existing Realms is high enough and if the new addition enhances the overall experience rather than diluting it.

> ***Q41***
> Are there any plans of changing or buffing any of the standard awakeners at the current juncture (Leigh and her atrocious health pool comes to mind. For someone who thrives off being smacked around, she makes an anemic leper look healthy.) 

***A41***
Yes. The battle designers will continue to enhance and adjust Standard Awakeners to ensure they maintain a stable presence in the current meta. We have recently been planning the next round of changes, which are expected to roll out gradually over the coming months.
As mentioned previously, we will also be introducing an additional system in the future to address the structural gap in base attributes for Standard Awakeners.
Once all of this is in place, we will continue to monitor Leigh's performance and make any further adjustments as necessary.

> ***Q42***
> Also, are there any more faction bonuses planned in the same vein as the Lemurians buffing each other — or was that very specifically implemented as a unit balance measure, and not one that will see much use outside of them?

***A42***
Currently, we don't have a concrete plan to extend faction bonuses to other factions, though it's not entirely out of the question in the long run.

Faction bonuses are first and foremost a narrative-driven design. For example, Murphy: Fauxborn belongs to Lemuria, while Murphy does not — this is closely tied to their respective attitudes toward and identities within Lemuria. We therefore wanted to express this difference through the mechanics.
From a gameplay perspective, th
is design also brought additional balancing benefits: on one hand, it prevents the "Murphy: Fauxborn + Murphy" sacrifice combo from becoming an overly prominent single optimal solution; on the other hand, it expands the overall presence of the Lemuria system. 

But these outcomes are still a natural byproduct of the design philosophy of "making every choice as meaningful as possible" — not restrictions forcibly imposed solely for the sake of balance.

If faction bonuses are made too strong, "maxing out the faction bonus" becomes the only correct answer, and players will clearly feel they are being "taught how to play" by the designers — which undermines their freedom in team building. This requires constant restraint and balance across three dimensions: power level, narrative, and player freedom.

> ***Q43***
> Are there any plans on adding any more Welfare Units that players can play around with and upgrade with relatively little investment (save time)? And, by extension, and other accessible genesis characters? (Such as a Genesis Lotan — because we love our Leviathans) — Whether introduced through Arc 2, or a spec ops?

***A43***
Currently, we have no plans to introduce new non-gacha Awakeners. While we will continue to release more Genesis characters, unfortunately none are planned to have special acquisition methods like the one used for Ramona: Timeworn.

The reason Ramona: Timeworn utilized a unique acquisition system was a result of "narrative priority": in the lore, she and Ramona share the same physical body, meaning that even through support battles or other methods, they would never appear on the same team at the same time. Given this lore-based constraint, it wouldn't be appropriate for her to be placed in the gacha pool.

Going forward, we will try as much as possible to increase in-game obtainable rewards within reasonable limits, and combine community events with some non-standard commemorative bonuses, so that non-paying players have more opportunities to obtain limited Awakeners and complete their progression more easily. We will also continue to update the reward supply in the exchange shop.

While we are unable to regularly provide new "welfare Awakeners" the way some gacha games do, we will do our best to ensure that even without spending money, players can still enjoy a complete and fun gaming experience at a relatively healthy pace.

> ***Q44***
> Are there any intentions on increasing the keeper level cap as the game continues to exist? 

***A44***
Originally, the Keeper Level was designed to showcase a player's accumulated progress before reaching the endgame. The cap was intended to be relatively easy to achieve, so that once players reached it, they wouldn't have to worry about the level itself anymore.

However, as more and more players reached the cap, many expressed a desire for it to be raised further. The system designers therefore re-evaluated its role: repositioning it as a form of clear, visible long-term accumulation. As a result, the Keeper Level cap has been raised twice since then.

In the future, if there is indeed a need as the game continues to develop, this may happen again. However, we will carefully manage the associated rewards, content unlocks, and power pressure, keeping them reasonable and acceptable for the majority of players.

**Q45
Are there any plans of allowing the player to better customize their starting screen. If only because I really like the Thais starting screen. **

A45
Some players have actually told me that Thais's login screen is too "overwhelming" and would like the option to switch back to an earlier one (laughs).

Regarding the request for a customizable login interface — the community has actually brought this up multiple times already, but the technical team has been busy with other development tasks and simply hasn't had time to address it yet.

Since you've brought it up again, I'll officially add it to the schedule as a small feature — please bear with us a little longer, it will happen!

> ***Q40***
> 24 serves a very unique role in the game as an awakener that actively, compliments all the realms — belonging to all of them in a certain sense. Are there any plans on adding other awakeners who 'belong' to multiple realms like her? 

***A40***
24 is a true exception among exceptions. Whether it is her dual-form switching in combat, her "Four Realms Adaptation" that shifts based on your team lineup, or her "Fragmentation and Reclamation" resonance in Special Ops — every mechanic serves to reinforce the narrative of her split personality. Even her trial stages and rewards come in four separate sets

As we've discussed regarding our character design philosophy: we didn't want to forcibly add a "four-realm adaptation" mechanic to the game and then go looking for a character to attach it to. On the contrary, it was precisely because we needed to present "24" as a character who truly works that we tailor-made this entire set of mechanics for her

This is almost equivalent to compressing four character niches into a single unit — it significantly eats into our future design space, and from a business rationality standpoint, it simply isn't cost-effective. Yet under the design philosophy of "unifying gameplay and narrative as much as possible," the battle designers chose to do it anyway. Even after so many practical compromises along the way, there have to be certain moments where you burn once for your ideals

Looking at the results, this design did incidentally solve some problems in the meta at the time — for instance, how the Chaos Realm's DPS role could interact with other Realms and present different playstyles. But ultimately, this was still a natural outcome driven by the design philosophy of "making every choice as interesting as possible.

Therefore, we currently have no plans to create more Awakeners of the same "multi-realm" type — unless a new character emerges in the future whose narrative expression truly requires this mechanic in order to work
